era_context: The early 14th century continues with the Papacy in transition toward Avignon, the Delhi Sultanate consolidating power, the Yuan Dynasty managing internal factions, and the Ottoman Empire beginning its rise in Anatolia. The Hundred Years' War between England and France is still in the future (begins 1337), but Anglo-French tensions are mounting. The Mali Empire is expanding in West Africa.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Oceania and Pacific Islands (minimal written records)
Americas (no written records in this period)

events.title: Clement V Elected Pope
events.description: Clement V (Bertrand de Got) is elected Pope in 1305, but by 1308 he is consolidating his position and preparing for the relocation of the papal seat. His election was heavily influenced by French King Philip IV, marking a significant shift in papal independence. Clement V's papacy is characterized by his close relationship with the French crown and his willingness to accommodate royal interests, including the suppression of the Knights Templar.
events.certainty_note: Clement V's election in 1305 and his early papacy are well-documented. By 1308, his policies are clearly established.

events.title: Knights Templar Arrests and Interrogations Continue
events.description: Following the mass arrests of Knights Templar on Friday, October 13, 1307, interrogations continue throughout 1308. Many Templars are tortured to extract confessions of heresy, idol worship, and other charges. The trials are orchestrated by King Philip IV to seize the order's vast wealth and eliminate a rival power center. By 1308, the extent of the conspiracy against the Templars is becoming clear to observers across Europe, though the papacy initially resists Philip's pressure.

sources.name: Trial records and confessions (many under torture)

events.certainty_note: The trials are extensively documented in contemporary records. The confessions are widely recognized as coerced.

events.title: Delhi Sultanate Consolidation Under Alauddin Khalji's Successors
events.description: The Delhi Sultanate, under the Khalji dynasty, continues to consolidate its vast territorial holdings. Alauddin Khalji died in 1316, but by 1308, his successors are managing the empire's administrative and military affairs. The sultanate is at the height of its territorial extent, controlling much of the Indian subcontinent. However, internal instability and succession disputes are beginning to emerge, foreshadowing the dynasty's decline.
events.certainty_note: The Delhi Sultanate's political situation in 1308 is inferred from broader historical context. Specific events for this year are not precisely documented.
cross_references: 1316 (death of Alauddin Khalji); relates to broader Delhi Sultanate history

events.title: Yuan Dynasty Internal Factionalism
events.description: The Yuan Dynasty continues to experience internal factionalism between Mongol aristocrats and Han Chinese officials. Emperor Wuzong (Kulug Khan) reigns from 1307 to 1311, a period marked by political instability and competing power centers. By 1308, the court is divided over administrative policies, with Confucian-educated officials advocating for Han Chinese governance practices while Mongol nobles resist sinicization.

sources.name: Yuan Shi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty)
Historiographic consensus on Yuan political history

events.certainty_note: Wuzong's reign is documented, but specific events in 1308 are not precisely dated. The general political situation is inferred from broader Yuan history.

events.title: Ottoman Early Expansion and Consolidation
events.description: The Ottoman beylik continues its gradual expansion in northwestern Anatolia under Osman I. By 1308, the Ottomans are consolidating control over former Byzantine and Seljuk territories through military campaigns and strategic alliances. The capture of Bursa (1326) remains in the future, but the foundation for Ottoman expansion is being laid. This period marks the transition from a small principality to a regional power.
events.certainty_note: Early Ottoman history is poorly documented in contemporary sources. Most accounts come from later Ottoman chronicles. The specific date of 1308 is not precisely marked.
cross_references: 1326 (capture of Bursa); relates to broader Ottoman rise

disconfirming_evidence: The narrative of the Knights Templar trials as purely a political conspiracy by Philip IV has been complicated by recent scholarship suggesting genuine concerns about the order's practices. Additionally, the characterization of the Khalji dynasty's decline as inevitable is challenged by some historians who note the sultanate's continued strength and administrative sophistication. The dating of early Ottoman expansion is highly uncertain; some sources place key events several years earlier or later than traditionally assigned.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1308 is heavily weighted toward Western European sources, particularly French royal records and papal documents. Asian sources are more sparse and often compiled centuries after the events. African and American histories are almost entirely absent from contemporary written records. The surviving sources reflect the biases of literate elites—court chronicles, religious institutions, and merchant records. Oral traditions and non-elite perspectives are largely invisible. The year 1308 is well-documented for Western Europe but only moderately documented for Asia and poorly documented for most other regions.

graph_edges.note: Clement V's consolidation of papal authority in 1308 leads to the relocation to Avignon in 1309
Templar trials continue from 1308 through 1309 and beyond
The consolidation period of 1308 precedes the Khalji dynasty's decline after 1316
Yuan factionalism in 1308 leads to the accession of Ayurbarwada in 1311
